content:
# Leviticus Chapter 16 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)

**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 67v–68r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_016_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_016_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** The LORD spoke to Moses after the death of Aaron's two sons — the ones who came near the LORD's presence and died. **2** The LORD said to Moses: "Tell your brother Aaron that he must not come whenever he chooses into the Most Holy Place, behind the curtain, before the mercy seat on the ark — or he will die. For I appear in the cloud over the mercy seat.

**3** "This is how Aaron may enter the Holy Place: with a young bull for a sin offering and a ram for a burnt offering. **4** He shall put on the holy linen tunic; linen undergarments shall cover his body; he shall tie on the linen sash and wrap the linen turban around his head. These are the sacred garments. Before putting them on, he shall bathe his whole body in water.

**5** "From the whole community of Israel he shall receive two male goats for a sin offering and one ram for a burnt offering.

**6** "Aaron shall bring the bull for his own sin offering and make atonement for himself and his household. **7** He shall then take the two goats and present them before the LORD at the entrance to the tent of meeting. **8** He shall cast lots over the two goats — one lot for the LORD and one lot for Azazel. **9** The goat on which the lot falls for the LORD, Aaron shall offer as a sin offering. **10** But the goat on which the lot falls for Azazel shall be stood alive before the LORD, to make atonement over it, and then sent off into the wilderness to Azazel.

**11** "Aaron shall bring the bull for his own sin offering and make atonement for himself and his household. He shall slaughter the bull for his own sin offering. **12** He shall take a censer full of burning coals from the altar before the LORD, and two handfuls of finely ground fragrant incense, and carry them behind the curtain. **13** He shall put the incense on the fire before the LORD, so that the cloud of incense covers the mercy seat over the covenant tablets — otherwise he will die. **14** He shall take some of the bull's blood and sprinkle it with his finger on the front of the mercy seat; then he shall sprinkle some of it with his finger seven times before the mercy seat.

**15** "He shall then slaughter the goat of the people's sin offering, bring its blood behind the curtain, and do with its blood what he did with the bull's blood: he shall sprinkle it on the mercy seat and before the mercy seat. **16** In this way he shall make atonement for the Most Holy Place, because of the ritual impurities of the Israelites and their acts of rebellion — all their sins. He shall do the same for the tent of meeting, which dwells among them in the midst of their impurity. **17** No one shall be in the tent of meeting from the time Aaron enters to make atonement in the Most Holy Place until he comes back out. He shall make atonement for himself, his household, and the whole assembled community of Israel.

**18** "Then he shall come out to the altar before the LORD and make atonement for it. He shall take some of the bull's blood and some of the goat's blood and put it on all four horns of the altar. **19** He shall sprinkle some of the blood on it with his finger seven times. In this way he shall cleanse it and consecrate it from the impurities of the Israelites.

**20** "When Aaron has finished making atonement for the Most Holy Place, the tent of meeting, and the altar, he shall bring forward the live goat. **21** He shall lay both his hands on the head of the live goat and confess over it all the iniquities of the Israelites, all their acts of rebellion, all their sins. He shall put them on the goat's head and send it away into the wilderness in the care of a man appointed for the task. **22** The goat will carry on itself all their iniquities to a desolate place, and he shall release the goat in the wilderness.

**23** "Aaron shall then come into the tent of meeting, take off the linen garments he put on before entering the Most Holy Place, and leave them there. **24** He shall bathe his body in water in a holy place, put his regular garments back on, and then come out and offer his own burnt offering and the burnt offering for the people, making atonement for himself and for the people. **25** He shall also burn the fat of the sin offering on the altar.

**26** "The man who released the Azazel goat shall wash his clothes, bathe his body in water, and afterward may come back into the camp. **27** The bull and the goat for the sin offerings — the ones whose blood was brought in to make atonement in the Most Holy Place — shall be carried outside the camp. Their hides, their flesh, and their dung shall be burned in the fire. **28** The man who burns them shall wash his clothes, bathe his body in water, and afterward may come back into the camp.

**29** "This shall be a permanent statute for you: In the seventh month, on the tenth day of the month, you shall humble yourselves and do no work at all — neither the native-born nor the foreigner who lives among you. **30** For on this day atonement will be made for you, to cleanse you. You will be clean from all your sins before the LORD. **31** It is a sabbath of complete rest for you, and you shall humble yourselves. It is a permanent statute.

**32** "The priest who is anointed and ordained to serve in his father's place shall make the atonement. He shall put on the sacred linen garments, **33** make atonement for the Most Holy Place, make atonement for the tent of meeting and the altar, and make atonement for the priests and all the people of the assembly. **34** This shall be a permanent statute for you: atonement shall be made once a year for all the sins of the Israelites."

And it was done as the LORD commanded Moses.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### The Day the World Depended On

Yom Kippur — the Day of Atonement — is the theological heartbeat of the entire book of Leviticus, and perhaps of the entire Mosaic covenant. Every other chapter circles this one. The sacrificial system, the priesthood, the purity laws, the regulations of the altar — all of it exists to maintain the conditions that make this single day possible; and this single day exists to reset those conditions for another year. Without the Day of Atonement, the accumulation of Israel's impurity would eventually make the divine presence unendurable. With it, the slate is wiped, and the LORD can remain in Israel's midst.

The chapter opens with a shadow of death: "after the death of Aaron's two sons" (v.1). Nadab and Abihu, who offered unauthorized fire before the LORD (Leviticus 10:1–2), are the reason Aaron cannot simply walk into the Most Holy Place whenever he chooses. The holiness of that space is not symbolic; it is lethal to the unprepared. The elaborate ritual of chapter 16 is not bureaucratic procedure — it is the survival manual for approaching a God who will not be approached casually.

### Two Goats: One Theology

The most extraordinary feature of this chapter is the two-goat ritual at its center (vv.7–10, 20–22). What is striking is that the two goats together accomplish what neither can accomplish alone.

The first goat dies. Its blood is taken behind the curtain — the only time in the year this is done — and sprinkled on and before the mercy seat. This is the blood-atonement: the guilt of Israel, which has contaminated the sanctuary, is addressed by blood on the place where the LORD meets Israel. The altar is cleansed, the tent is cleansed, the Holy of Holies itself is cleansed. The mechanism of defilement is reversed. This goat covers the problem as it affects the *sanctuary*.

The second goat lives. It is not slaughtered; it is confessed over. Aaron lays both hands — the full-weight gesture of complete transference — on its head and recites the catalogue of Israel's sins: iniquities, rebellions, sins. The goat then leaves, carrying the confession into the wilderness, to a desolate and uninhabited place, to Azazel. This goat addresses the problem as it affects the *people*. Their sins are not merely covered; they are removed. Carried away. Gone.

The two goats together accomplish what no single image can: *substitutionary death and complete removal*. The blood of the first goat says: guilt has been addressed at cost, met with death, satisfied. The departure of the second goat says: and it is gone, carried to the wilderness, never to return. One theology, two dramatic acts.

The writer of Hebrews will exploit this architecture in chapters 9–10, arguing that Christ performs both roles permanently and finally: his blood enters the true Most Holy Place (not the earthly tent), and his sacrifice, offered once rather than annually, accomplishes what the annual ritual could only enact symbolically. The Day of Atonement is the shadow; the reality casts that shadow.

### Azazel: The Irreducible Mystery

The word *Azazel* (vv.8, 10, 26) is one of the most debated terms in all of biblical Hebrew. Four main interpretations have circulated across centuries of scholarship:

1. **A place name** — a remote desert location, perhaps meaning "rugged terrain" or "rocky cliff." On this reading, the goat is simply sent to the wilderness, and "Azazel" marks the destination.

2. **A desert demon** — a malevolent supernatural being associated with the wilderness, to whom the sins of the community are symbolically returned. The Second Temple book of *1 Enoch* develops this reading extensively, making Azazel a fallen angel who taught humanity forbidden arts. The name appears in other Second Temple texts in this sense.

3. **An abstract concept** — from *az* (goat) + *azal* (to go away), the whole phrase meaning "the goat that goes away," which is simply "scapegoat" in its literal, etymological sense. This reading makes Azazel a label for the ritual itself, not a separate entity.

4. **Complete removal** — a compound meaning "entire sending off" or "complete destruction," emphasizing the finality of the sin-removal.

The grammatical structure of verse 8 — "one lot for the LORD and one lot for Azazel" — places Azazel in syntactic parallel with the LORD. This is the most theologically uncomfortable reading, because it seems to imply Azazel is an entity, not merely a place or a concept, and that the goat is being offered *to* that entity. Most interpreters resist this conclusion, but it is grammatically available. The text preserves the ambiguity rather than resolving it.

What the text clearly *does* say is this: the goat bearing Israel's sins goes *away* — out of the community, into the desolate places, beyond the inhabited world. Whether Azazel names a demon, a cliff, a concept, or a place, the directional meaning is the same: sin is removed from the people and sent in the opposite direction from the divine sanctuary. The sins leave. This is the point the narrative insists upon.

The translation here follows the near-universal practice of leaving Azazel untranslated. Substituting "scapegoat" — the English word that comes from William Tyndale's 1530 rendering — would be both etymologically questionable and theologically flattening. The mystery in the Hebrew deserves to remain mysterious in English.

### The High Priest's Vulnerability

One of the most arresting features of the chapter is the care taken to protect Aaron from dying (vv.2, 13). The danger is real and explicit. The incense cloud in verse 13 is not mere ceremony — it is a literal smoke screen between Aaron and the unveiled mercy seat, where the LORD appears. Without it, Aaron dies.

This creates a remarkable tension. On the Day of Atonement, the high priest is the most powerful ritual figure in Israel — the only person who may enter the Most Holy Place, the only person who may manipulate the blood of atonement on the ark, the mediator of the entire nation's reconciliation with God. And yet he enters clothed not in his normal splendid vestments (the breastplate, the ephod, the gold-adorned garments of Exodus 28) but in plain white linen. No gold. No jewels. Just the simplest possible priestly dress.

This stripping down is the required uniform for approach to the unapproachable. The high priest lays aside the emblems of his office and stands before God as a man, covered by nothing but linen and the smoke of incense. The elaborate gold vestments that announce his dignity before the community are precisely what he must remove when approaching the divine presence. Before God, the title and the regalia do not help. What helps is following the exact protocol of approach — the washing, the incense, the blood — and even then, the survival is by grace, not by merit.

### The Sanctuary as Patient

A strange and easily missed feature of verses 15–19 is that the primary subject of atonement is not the people — it is the sanctuary. "He shall make atonement for the Most Holy Place" (v.16). "He shall make atonement for the tent of meeting and the altar" (v.33). The building needs cleansing. The altar needs blood.

The logic is precise. Israel's sins and impurities do not only affect Israel; they defile the place where God dwells among them. The sanctuary is contaminated by the spiritual state of the community it serves. As Jacob Milgrom and others have argued, the biblical worldview imagines sin and impurity as forces that travel through the community and accumulate at the sanctuary — attracted to the holy place, as impurity is always drawn toward its opposite. Year by year, this moral contamination builds up. The Day of Atonement is the annual purge, not primarily of the people's guilt-feelings but of the actual defilement that their sins have deposited in the LORD's house.

This is why the blood of the sacrifice is brought to the innermost room and applied to the mercy seat itself. The atonement must reach the center of the contamination. Purging the outer court would not be enough. The defilement has penetrated all the way in; the purification must travel all the way in to meet it.

### *Shabbat Shabbaton*: The Strongest Sabbath

Verse 31 uses the Hebrew phrase *shabbat shabbaton* — "a sabbath of complete rest" — the most emphatic sabbath formula in the Torah. The same expression appears only in the weekly Sabbath regulations (Exodus 31:15, 35:2) and in Leviticus 23:3 for Yom Kippur. It is the superlative form: not merely a rest day, but a rest of rests, a sabbath of utmost seriousness.

On this day, Israel does no work. The community comes to a complete halt. But the command does not merely say "rest"; it says "humble yourselves" (*initem nafshoteikhem* — literally "afflict your souls"). The traditional understanding is that this means fasting, though the verb can encompass a broader posture of self-denial and contrition. The idea is that the Day of Atonement is not a vacation day; it is a day of communal reckoning, a day to let the weight of the year's accumulation of sin register, to sit in the discomfort of mortality and fallibility before the God who can make it clean.

The combination of complete rest and self-humbling is theologically important. Rest without contrition risks becoming complacency. Contrition without rest risks becoming anxious striving. Together, they model the posture of a people who neither pretend everything is fine nor frantically try to fix it by their own effort. They stop. They acknowledge. They wait. The atonement is made — not by their industry, but by the high priest's blood, on their behalf, in the room they cannot enter.

### The Permanent Statute: Time and Repetition

The phrase "permanent statute" appears three times in this chapter (vv.29, 31, 34), marking this day as non-negotiable, non-negotiable across every generation. The repetition is insistent. This is not a temporary provision while Israel matures past its need for ritual. It is the permanent shape of life before the holy God.

The writer of Hebrews will argue precisely against this permanence — or rather, will argue that the annual repetition was itself the evidence of incompleteness: "If [the sacrifices] could have been made perfect, they would have stopped being offered; worshipers would no longer feel guilty for their sins" (Hebrews 10:2). The permanent, repeated nature of Yom Kippur is, from the Hebrews perspective, a built-in testimony to its own provisionality. Every year the high priest goes back in. Every year the goats are required. Every year the blood must be freshly applied. This regularity points forward to the one-for-all act that would render the annual cycle complete.

But within its own terms — within the covenant structure of Israel's life with God — the permanence is the point. Every tenth day of the seventh month, without fail, the accumulated weight of a year's sin and impurity is cleared. The slate is wiped. The LORD remains in the midst of his people. The camps remains habitable by the holy. The year can begin again.

### The Two Men Who Touch the Outside

Verses 26 and 28 introduce two unnamed functionaries: the man who leads the Azazel goat into the wilderness, and the man who burns the carcasses outside the camp. Both must wash before returning. Both are engaged in acts of removal — carrying away from the community what the ritual has displaced. The sin-bearing goat, the sin-laden carcasses, the defilements of the Day: they are physically removed, and those who remove them are temporarily excluded before being readmitted.

The pattern of temporary exclusion followed by washing and readmission mirrors the purity system of chapters 11–15. Contact with what has borne sin does not permanently contaminate — but it requires ritual acknowledgment and cleansing. The man who handles sin-removing and death-bearing things must pass through a threshold before re-entering the community. There is a boundary between the inside and the outside, between the holy camp and the wilderness beyond, and whoever crosses it — even in service of the holy — must cross back through water.

The spatial logic of the whole chapter maps along this inside/outside axis. The blood goes inward, to the innermost room. The goat goes outward, to the uninhabited wilderness. The carcasses go outward, beyond the camp. The high priest's entire journey is an arc from outside (at the altar), through the holy place, to the innermost room (the Holy of Holies), and back out again — progressively deeper, then progressively out. The geometry of atonement is centripetal followed by centrifugal: first the blood drawn inward to the place where God meets Israel, then the sin driven outward to the place where nothing dwells.

### LXX Observations

The Septuagint (Codex Vaticanus, pp. 102–103) renders *Azazel* as *apopompaios* — "the one sent away" — following the scapegoat / complete-removal interpretation rather than reading Azazel as a proper name for an entity. The LXX translators thus chose the de-mystifying reading, assimilating the term into a comprehensible ritual description. The theological consequence is that the uncomfortable syntactic parallel between "the LORD" and "Azazel" in the Hebrew (one lot for each) is softened; "the one sent away" is clearly a functional description, not a personal name.

The Greek word *exapostello* ("to send away completely") appears repeatedly in the Azazel goat passages, a compound verb emphasizing the thoroughness of the removal. This is the same word used in Galatians 4:4 — "God sent forth his Son" — where the idea of purposeful sending from the divine into the wilderness of human flesh carries something of the same weight. The one sent out bears what the community cannot bear on its own.

The LXX's rendering of *kapporet* as *hilasterion* — "place of propitiation/expiation" — is picked up verbatim in Romans 3:25, where Paul says that God put forward Christ as a *hilasterion*, "a mercy seat" or "propitiation." Paul's Greek-speaking readers, familiar with Leviticus 16 through the LXX, would have immediately understood the reference: Christ is the new mercy seat, the place where the divine presence meets human guilt, and blood is applied for atonement. The Day of Atonement is not merely an analogy Paul reaches for; it is the specific ritual his language evokes, word for word, from a chapter his readers knew.

---
*PNEUMA — translated from Leningrad Codex (B19A) folios 67v–68r, with reference to Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) pages 102–103 (LXX).*
